items.question: An interviewer asks: what are the three axioms that define a norm on a vector space, and which of them fails if you use ||v|| = max(|v₁|, |v₂|) without the absolute value on scalars?
items.answer: The three axioms are positive definiteness, absolute homogeneity (||cv|| = |c|·||v||), and the triangle inequality. If you forgot absolute value on c and used ||cv|| = c·||v|| for negative c, absolute homogeneity would fail because the left side stays non-negative while the right side could be negative.

items.question: You are building a RAG pipeline with sentence embeddings. The interviewer asks why you might choose cosine similarity over Euclidean distance—what is your answer?
items.answer: Cosine similarity measures alignment of direction and is insensitive to vector magnitude, which matters when embedding length correlates with document size rather than semantics. Euclidean distance on unnormalized embeddings can rank a long irrelevant document closer than a short relevant one if magnitudes differ. I would normalize embeddings or use cosine, then validate neighbors on a held-out query set.

items.question: Given x = (3, -4), compute ||x||₁, ||x||₂ and ||x||∞ and explain how each would change if you replaced x with -x.
items.answer: ||x||₁ = 7, ||x||₂ = 5, ||x||∞ = 4. Replacing x with -x leaves all three norms unchanged because L1 and L2 use absolute values or squares, and L∞ uses max absolute coordinate.

items.question: A teammate's k-NN model performs poorly on mixed-scale features. How do norms and preprocessing explain the failure, and what do you fix?
items.answer: L2 distance sums squared coordinate differences, so a feature with large numeric range dominates unless columns are scaled. I would inspect feature ranges, apply per-column standardization or min-max scaling before distance computation, and re-check nearest neighbors on known queries to confirm sensible rankings.
